posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
DavidZ:
ybarselah:

i’ve never understood producers that claim to be natural, terroir driven, bio, etc., - basically saying they care about the land and environment, and then ship massively heavy bottles around the world.

What does one have to do with the other? The climate impact of bottles is de minimis compared to the climate impact of viticulture.

the weight. these bottles are significantly heavier. and given the climate impact on viticulture (which i presume conscientious producers intend to minimize) there’s a ripe opportunity to completely avoid extra cost and impact when moving their product around, especially when that choice is inconsequential to the final product.

so, a case of wine can be as light as perhaps 35 pounds, or as heavy as 50+ pounds. that’s a massive delta that is completely avoidable, and therefore should be. to say nothing of the increase cost to everyone in the chain for shipping.

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked:
Martin Steinley:
DavidZ:

The climate impact of bottles is de minimis compared to the climate impact of viticulture.

I am pretty sure that the latter is necessary to produce wine. A f*ck!ng five pound bottle is not necessary to package it. It is stupid and utterly irresponsible.

Irresponsible? It’s basically symbolic. If you want to be environmentally conscious, don’t drink wine from cool climate places. Viticulture is MUCH friendlier to the environment in warmer climates. Burgundy and Beaujolais would be great places to cut out of your drinking arsenal if you want to encourage environmentally responsible winemaking.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: David, I am not talking about environmentally-friendly winemaking, which is a separate, complicated subject. I am talking about packaging, which is simple. Put the f*ck!ng wine in bottles that run about 35lbs. per case, rather than 50-55lbs. per case. It is not complicated and it is not difficult, and to do otherwise is, like I said, stupid and irresponsible, whether symbolic or not.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Before you guys all hook into Pierre-Yves you may like to know why he uses such bottles. We are all crying out for a solution to premox and Pierre-Yves is taking steps to address the problem. He believes that the glass could be one of the factors and has special bottles with long necks designed so the the cork can seat properly. I think he is also suspicious that re-cycled glass could be an issue. A couple of cows farts probably cause more damage to the environment than a few thousand heavier bottles in the name of premox prevention.

posts.name: Bill Tex Landreth
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:

Before you guys all hook into Pierre-Yves you may like to know why he uses such bottles. We are all crying out for a solution to premox and Pierre-Yves is taking steps to address the problem. He believes that the glass could be one of the factors and has special bottles with long necks designed so the the cork can seat properly. I think he is also suspicious that re-cycled glass could be an issue. A couple of cows farts probably cause more damage to the environment than a few thousand heavier bottles in the name of premox prevention.

Recycled glass? That is about the most ridiculous thing ever posted here.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
I have seen some more ridiculous things Bill.

Don’t shoot the messenger, just trying to pass on the gist of a conversation we had with him in April. There is no doubt that the corks have different levels of permeationand/or ability to seal a bottle in premox wine. The make-up of the glass and how a cork seals against it could be a factor could it not?

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:

I have seen some more ridiculous things Bill.

Don’t shoot the messenger, just trying to pass on the gist of a conversation we had with him in April. There is no doubt that the corks have different levels of permeationand/or ability to seal a bottle in premox wine. The make-up of the glass and how a cork seals against it could be a factor could it not?

Jeremy, with all due respect, these bottles are not about longer necks. They may be a quarter of an inch longer than a regular bottle, but they weigh about twice as much. Moreover, premox did not develop when producers moved away from five pound bottles. I am not buying Pierre-Yves’ pitch. Surely, Jean-Paul Brun is not trying to combat premox with his four pound bottle from which I poured tonight. These guys should be called out on this nonsense.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:

Before you guys all hook into Pierre-Yves you may like to know why he uses such bottles. We are all crying out for a solution to premox and Pierre-Yves is taking steps to address the problem. He believes that the glass could be one of the factors and has special bottles with long necks designed so the the cork can seat properly. I think he is also suspicious that re-cycled glass could be an issue. A couple of cows farts probably cause more damage to the environment than a few thousand heavier bottles in the name of premox prevention.

Seconded.

Roulot is also investigating the recycled glass question, in conjunction with a chemist if I recall correctly. Seems around the mid-1990s EU regulations mandated the inclusion of a certain percentage of recycled glass in the raw materials for bottle production. The idea is that that may have resulted in traces of metal oxides, which catalyze oxidation, getting into the glass. Now that may sound far-fetched, but if only more producers took such a thorough interest in premox and preventing it!

posts.name: Odd Rydland
posts.cooked: I seem to have read tests that show that (the same) wine scored higher served from heavier bottles than from lighter ones. Cannot find the reference though. What’s certain is that the mind is a complicated thing, and that most of us, including me, is subject to a lot of biases etc.

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked:
Martin Steinley:

David, I am not talking about environmentally-friendly winemaking, which is a separate, complicated subject. I am talking about packaging, which is simple. Put the f*ck!ng wine in bottles that run about 35lbs. per case, rather than 50-55lbs. per case. It is not complicated and it is not difficult, and to do otherwise is, like I said, stupid and irresponsible, whether symbolic or not.

But this is an idiotic place to draw the line. Why not package the wines in plastic bags, which are indisputably inert and provide for excellent seals. Think like a Capri Sun drink, scaled up. That would save far more weight than a lighter bottle.

Arguing for your own personal preference under a thin veil of environmental rationalization is anti-environment AND anti-wine. It manages to be harmful to everyone and cries wolf about an insignificant problem, making it harder to effect change for real issues. You should be ashamed of yourself, Martin.

posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:

Before you guys all hook into Pierre-Yves you may like to know why he uses such bottles. We are all crying out for a solution to premox and Pierre-Yves is taking steps to address the problem. He believes that the glass could be one of the factors and has special bottles with long necks designed so the the cork can seat properly. I think he is also suspicious that re-cycled glass could be an issue. A couple of cows farts probably cause more damage to the environment than a few thousand heavier bottles in the name of premox prevention.

I appreciate the longer necks and the longer corks and the wax capsule. And I think it’s ironic that he and Roulot would be the ones really looking into premox when they are 2 of the producers with the better premox record. But I’m all for it.

The new bottles are much wider in girth than the previous bottles (at least back to 2002) which barely fit into some racking systems as it were. This is the first significant change in bottle girth. I think they may be as wide as Krug bottles now. Something to consider when choosing which kind of racking system to use for your cellar. The wine store where I purchased these from shredded the label of one bottle trying to fit it into their shelving.

Maybe it’s due to a new distributor/manufacturer for his bottles?

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
DavidZ:

If you want to be environmentally conscious, don’t drink wine from cool climate places. Viticulture is MUCH friendlier to the environment in warmer climates. Burgundy and Beaujolais would be great places to cut out of your drinking arsenal if you want to encourage environmentally responsible winemaking.

How do you figure that? Because of treatments for mold and mildew? Irrigation is far more common in warm-hot climates than in cool climates, and the former are often the places where water is most scarce.

The idea is that that may have resulted in traces of metal oxides, which catalyze oxidation, getting into the glass. Now that may sound far-fetched, but if only more producers took such a thorough interest in premox and preventing it!

Thanks for the information, guys. I shouldn’t have judged without knowing the reason. I don’t see how this would explain why premox is so much more common in Burgundy than anywhere else, but it does seem like it’s worth exploring.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
DavidZ:
Martin Steinley:

David, I am not talking about environmentally-friendly winemaking, which is a separate, complicated subject. I am talking about packaging, which is simple. Put the f*ck!ng wine in bottles that run about 35lbs. per case, rather than 50-55lbs. per case. It is not complicated and it is not difficult, and to do otherwise is, like I said, stupid and irresponsible, whether symbolic or not.

But this is an idiotic place to draw the line. Why not package the wines in plastic bags, which are indisputably inert and provide for excellent seals. Think like a Capri Sun drink, scaled up. That would save far more weight than a lighter bottle.

Arguing for your own personal preference under a thin veil of environmental rationalization is anti-environment AND anti-wine. It manages to be harmful to everyone and cries wolf about an insignificant problem, making it harder to effect change for real issues. You should be ashamed of yourself, Martin.

Plastic bags are not indisputably inert, and the current technology for bag in box or anything like that does not provide an excellent seal at all. It’s extremely tough to stabilize wines with residual sugar that are packaged that way because the bags do let a tiny amount of air through. That’s also why those packages aren’t good for much more than a year (some manufacturers claim 2 years, but studies have shown noticeable oxidation well before that), so they’re really not feasible for any wine with the potential to age.

Put the f*ck!ng wine in bottles that run about 35lbs. per case, rather than 50-55lbs. per case. It is not complicated and it is not difficult, and to do otherwise is, like I said, stupid and irresponsible, whether symbolic or not.

But this is an idiotic place to draw the line. Why not package the wines in plastic bags, which are indisputably inert and provide for excellent seals. Think like a Capri Sun drink, scaled up. That would save far more weight than a lighter bottle.

Arguing for your own personal preference under a thin veil of environmental rationalization is anti-environment AND anti-wine. It manages to be harmful to everyone and cries wolf about an insignificant problem, making it harder to effect change for real issues. You should be ashamed of yourself, Martin.

Plastic bags are not indisputably inert, and the current technology for bag in box or anything like that does not provide an excellent seal at all. It’s extremely tough to stabilize wines with residual sugar that are packaged that way because the bags do let a tiny amount of air through. That’s also why those packages aren’t good for much more than a year (some manufacturers claim 2 years, but studies have shown noticeable oxidation well before that), so they’re really not feasible for any wine with the potential to age.

That’s correct for “bag in box”. Not correct for bags generally. There are more expensive bags (which defeat the purpose of bag-in-box technology, FWIW) that are superior. Flexible packaging is almost always the most environmentally-friendly solution, because its lighter and can be transported in smaller spaces.

Alcohol is an organic solvent. I doubt even the expensive bags can store alcohol for decades without some effect.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked: If glass were a culprit, why would only burgundian white wine be affected? I presume the glass industry that serves burgundy also serves other areas? Why not premox Alsace ? N rhone? Etc.

posts.name: Robert Love
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:

Before you guys all hook into Pierre-Yves you may like to know why he uses such bottles. We are all crying out for a solution to premox and Pierre-Yves is taking steps to address the problem. He believes that the glass could be one of the factors and has special bottles with long necks designed so the the cork can seat properly. I think he is also suspicious that re-cycled glass could be an issue. A couple of cows farts probably cause more damage to the environment than a few thousand heavier bottles in the name of premox prevention.

While I agree this sounds ridiculous on its face, it has more explanatory power than many of the other theories put forward (changes in bâtonnage really?) that fail to explain why the issue blanketed the industry so uniformly starting at the same time.

I believe I first heard this argument from Allen Meadows. The two interesting facts are that the EU regulation took effect in 1995 and that there is believed to be a higher concentration of copper in the glass as a result.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:

If glass were a culprit, why would only burgundian white wine be affected? I presume the glass industry that serves burgundy also serves other areas? Why not premox Alsace ? N rhone? Etc.

I’ve encountered premoxed Clos St Hune and Haut Brion blanc, just to pick two pinnacles. Northern Rhone gets let off because of all that talk about the wines going through ‘an oxidative phase’.

Clearly the problem is multifactorial however, but perhaps this is one more tesera for the mosaic.
